1. Groups

1.1. Motivation and definition

  • Groups are used to describe symmetry and transformations.
  • Def Let be a set. Define as the set of bijective maps .

  • A group of transformations of is a subset satisfying the following relations:

    1. Closure: .
    2. Identity: where .
    3. Inverse : .

    • Examples

      1. .
      2. .
      3. , the set of invertible linear operators.
      4. , the set of rotational symmetries of an -gon.

      5. , (Dihedral group) the set of all symmetries (rotations and reflections) of an -gon.

        • There are of both, making this an order group.
      6. group of distance preserving operators (isometries).
    • Many group of transformations come from taking transformations that preserve symmetry, e.g. linearity, structure of polygon, and distance.

  • A group is a set equipped with a multiplication satisfying the following relations

    1. Associativity: a(b c) = (a b) c.
    2. Identity:
    3. Inverse: .
    • A pair only satisfying 1 or 1&2 is called a semigroup or monoid resp.

  • Prop. The alternating group is generated by 3-cycles.

    • Prop. There is no subgroup of with order . Every subgroup of index 2 is normal. Since all the 3-cycles would be mapped to identity, contradicting Theorem 2.12.2
  • Prop. An -cycle can be expressed transpositions of neighbors: . Thus the sign is
  • Game of 15: Invariant is sign of permutation times where is the row number. (Horizontal moves obviously don’t change it. Vertical moves adds 4-cycle which is odd)
    Exercise: Prove for an x board for and that any two positions of the same sign can always be reached from each other. Hint: Do 2x3 case by hand and then use induction.

1.6. Cosets

  • Def A partition of a set is a subdivision of into disjoint nonempty subsets.

  • Def An equivalence relation is a binary relation is transitive, symmetric, and reflexive.

    • Examples:

      1. Cosets ( if for some )
      2. Conjugacy ( if for some )
      3. Equivalence relation defined by a map:
  • Let be the surjective map that maps to its equivalence class, denoted or .

  • Define the fiber of an element under a function as its preimage.

    • Prop. Let be the kernel of a homomorphsim . Then the fiber of that contains is the coset .

  • Prop. The left cosets of a subgroup partition .

    • The equivalence relation for left cosets is if for some .
  • Lem. All left cosets have the same order.

  • Thm. (Lagrange) Let the index of a subgroup be the number of left cosets. Then

    (6)

    • Cor. The order of an element of a finite group divides the order of the group.
    • Cor. Groups of prime order are cyclic.
    • Example:

  • Left and right cosets aren’t necessarily the same.

    • Prop. If is normal then the left and right cosets are equal.
    • Prop. The number of left and right cosets are equal
      Proof. is a left and right coset. Since all left and right cosets have order equal to , by Lagrange’s theorem there must be the same number of left and right cosets.
    • Prop. If the left and right cosets are the same, then they are necessarily in the form and for some .
      Proof. Suppose the left coset is . This contains . Since the right cosets are disjoint, the only right coset that contains this is .
  • Thm. Correspondence theorem: Suppose is a surjective homomorphism. There is a bijective mapping between subgroups of containing the kernel of and subgroups in .

1.7. Structure of groups

  1. Groups of prime order are cyclic.

  2. Groups of order where and are relatively prime integers are isomorphic to product of and .

    • Proved later with Sylow theorem
    • Example . This is exactly the Chinese remainder theorem. An isomorphism is

    (7)

  3. Structure of group of order 4 is either isomorphic to or the Klein four group .

1.8. Quotient Groups

  • Alternate definition of normal subgroup; left cosets equal right cosets

    • Examples:

      1. is normal in for all .

        • This is because kernel of sign homomorphism is precisely and see example 3.
      2. Prop. Every subgroup of index is normal.
        Proof. https://proofwiki.org/wiki/Subgroup_of_Index_2_is_Normal
      3. Prop. Kernel of any homomorphism is normal.
      4. Prop. Center of is a normal subgroup
      5. Exercise: Let be the group of motions (distance preserving transformations of consisting of translations and rotations about any point. Let be the subgroup of translations and be the subgroup of rotations about the origin. Then is normal but is not normal.)

  • Normal subgroups are important because their cosets combine nicely and allow us to define quotient groups.

    • Let be the set of cosets of normal subgroup .
    • Prop. If is normal, the product set equals .

    • Prop. equipped with the law of composition forms a group.

      • Def. Let the quotient group be the set of cosets equipped with such composition.

    • Thm. For normal , given by is a surjective homomorphism whose kernel is . This is called the canonical map from to .

      • Prop. .
      • Cor. Every normal subgroup is the kernel of a surjective homomorphism.

    • Exercise: Show that if is not normal, then there is no group structure on such that defined above is a homomorphism.

      • For example, it is easy to verify that is not normal. There is no group structure in the set of left cosets with a surjective homomorphism . Indeed, , so if it is given a group structure it will be a group . But any homomorphism is trivial (maps to 0) since is generated by elements of order and every non-identity element in has order 3 (write out homomorphism).

1.9. Conjugacy classes

  • Prop. Conjugacy is an equivalency relation.
  • Prop. Conjugation by an element of is an automorphism. We call it an inner automorphism.
